summary: McKenzie argues that software developers systematically underprice their products due to psychological biases rather than market reality. He contends that customers don't know or care how long software took to build — they only care whether it solves their problem. Using the analogy of a heart surgeon who charges $50,000 for implanting $5 worth of parts, he demonstrates that value-based pricing is the norm in professional services. He explains that software's near-zero marginal cost means developers can pick any point on the demand curve, and that the demand curve is external to development effort. The core message is that developers compare their shipped product to their original vision and feel it falls short, leading them to underprice — a phenomenon he calls 'coder's remorse.'
key_points: Customers can only hold development time against you if they know about it — there's no reason to volunteer that information
Non-developer customers see software as black magic and will pay for working solutions rather than attempt to build their own
The demand curve is external to you — it doesn't shift based on how much time or money you spent on development
Value-based pricing means your price should reflect what the customer gets out of it, not what it cost you to make
Developers systematically undervalue their own software by comparing it to their original imagined ideal rather than to what actually exists in the market
money_quotes: Your heart surgeon doesn't tell you he's billing you $50,000 for three hours' labor to implant 32 cents worth of plastic and a $5 chip made in Taiwan.
Everything that happens inside of the computer is black magic to them and you are the shaman they rely on for magical amulets to keep the evil spirits at bay.
The demand curve doesn't magically shift up or shift down just because you spent 10 years or $3 million on the development effort — the demand curve is external to you.
Regardless of what it took to make my product, my price is a 'fair' one because you'll get oh so much more out of it.
I know of at least one product out there which would probably see higher sales if it were priced at triple what it is right now.
There is no competitor who released your ImaginationWare into the market — you're competing only with what actually exists, not with what could exist.
key_insight: Software pricing should be based on the value customers receive, not on development cost or time, and most developers underprice because they compare their product to an imagined ideal rather than to the actual market.
